sentences.text: I could-- because the light is hot.
It's hot.
OK.
Yes.
Well, it was a-- I don't know how you call it.
We called it [INAUDIBLE].
That's [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], we call it.
Music and dancing.
Jewish people were dancing and drinking a lot that time.
They knew it is something going on.
That young-- I was young, and young crowd they were enjoying themselves so much.
And my father decide to take us.
Because it was that same building downstairs.
To take us to that [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
That was first time I'd been in that night.
Like a first time I spent in musician.
And it was-- I was fascinated.
Well, it was end of '42, because we married in '43 very short time after we met.
And I met my husband.
I seen these two musician.
One was violinist-- it was his brother, Henry.
He was much older.
And there-- but actually, first-- my first date, what I-- later I can call that.
What was his younger brother-- Samek, my husband younger brother.
He's-- he's not alive.
He died during the war from-- from hunger, I believe.
Because it was not easy to get to musician like that.
He was such a-- for me, it was something.
A very important man playing accordion.
And he got blue eyes.
I was mad about blue eyes.
And he was playing.
And he goes around and asked whatever we wanted that night.
Ask for some song.
It wasn't-- the name of the song, I remember.
It was, Nobody's Lonely-- Longing For Me, or something like that.
And he play for me it.
Yes.
I can't sing, because I'm not musical.
From But I remember the word.
That was one thing, what I find out that I'm not musical.
Because I always thought that I am.
I always thought that I can sing.
But when first I start to something, my husband-- ah.
That was his reaction.
And that shut me up for all my married life.
I'm not musical, but luckily, my true children are.
And my grandchildren are very musical.
But I'm really lucky.
That was my wish that they take after that.
Yes, I met him.
Through his brother I met him.
And we start to-- meeting.
Well, it was nowhere to go.
The date was on the balcony.
That was a big balcony.
It was a bit darker, and we start to kiss.
And I enjoyed that.
And-- well-- after sometime, I said, what about we decided to get married?
He lives in the ghetto with his sister.
And the sister-- sister-in-law.
Leo's sister-in-law and brother, Henry.
Did they live near you?
Yes.
And lived in the same apartment-- not the same apartment, but the same complex of apartments.
Because it was a huge building.
A very big building.
And, well, we kiss in the balcony.
I remember this.
And when we went to his sister's, she said, you [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
What?
What?
You're going to be married.
Here you don't know what you'll eat tomorrow.
And I said, it doesn't matter.
Whatever we have, we will share it.
And we get married.
We had a rabbi.
And my sister-- another sister-in-law.
Manci, she [INAUDIBLE] from the brother or something.
I got a suit, and I had little hat, because his sister was modest.
And little veil, a small veil.
And it was-- but his parents were not there already.
Not my father, because they were-- in June '42, that was selection, and they went.
Taken by German transport.
My father and Leo's parents-- father and mother.
That we didn't have nobody.
But his uncle was still in the ghetto, and he was the one who lead us to rabbi.
That's how we have our wedding.
And even my sister-in-law make some like dinner or whatever.
And actually, the next day he went to Plaszow.
That we heard-- in the ghetto, we knew that is somewhere built a concentration camp.
Yeah.
OK.
No.
Well, when we started living in ghetto, that was again-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] order.
It to a-- some big placard on everywhere what Jews not allowed to do.
And that was segregation.
And there were-- German coming.
First of all, elderly people.
He wasn't old.
He was 53, my father.
And most probably my husband's parents were not much older.
The same age.
But that-- that means old for German.
And they took them to the appellplatz.
You know, the appellplatz like a cart-- like a market.
And they took them on the wagon, and they went away.
My father said, don't worry it.
Most probably I will go and fight.
Most probably I will get a gun, because he was very-- he was a solider.
And he didn't believe it that he's going to die.
Nobody knew it but-- nobody believed.
It was a lot of people at that time.
That was in June, I remember.
And we met in June '42.
It was June '42 when he was taken away.
When they went, yes.
That was one of the selection, because there was a few selection like this.
But that one particular I remember, because my father went and his parents.
And we met after this.
They already start to build Plaszow.
We find we knew about that-- that's-- because, well, they all left me after my marriage.
No.
No, I never seen him.
That was-- that was the last time.
I know now.
I knew after the war.
But at that time, I didn't know.
No, no.
He was very brave.
He knew that he's going to fight.
He's going to war.
He didn't-- he didn't-- didn't imagine that he will go for his death.
And it was a big, big transport.
There were a few transport like this.
Children, young children.
Sick people from hospital.
They liquidate all hospital.
Because in the ghetto, it was everything.
It was one chemist shop which was Christian.
That was only one.
[NON-ENGLISH] was his name.
Because his chemist shop was within the ghetto, and they live him up there.
Actually, he was writing a book in Polish about ghetto, Cracow ghetto.
All the others were Jewish.
But it was-- there were-- people were working.
Tailor, or shoemaker, or shop was open in the beginning.
Then they start to cut it.
They start to send people away.
Ghetto was smaller and smaller.
And on the other hand, there are some people who come from other ghettos from country side.
That again was crowded.
Then again, it was such a-- such a time that we didn't know what was happening.
And how I didn't know what was happening.
It must be probably some [INAUDIBLE] or listen to some wireless or something.
I didn't know much about it.
And that was a time that the ghetto was-- they were catching us to work on the railway to clean that.
And when it's winter, we have to sweep that snow.
And they sent people from ghetto to work.
All different kind of the work.
In a column you have to walk, and German was on each side on it.
And we walk.
That's what I was doing.
Once, I went-- a few times I went to clean the street.
Have a shovel and clean the street when it was cold.
And-- well, that's all different things what I have to do.
What they ask-- what they ask us to do.
It was in-- oh, many.
It was many.
With us lived one couple, [?
Bochinksi. ?] That where the other people-- the big flat.
It was big room.
And it was divided by wardrobes.
With wardrobes to have a little privacy, and the other have another cupboard to have a little privacy, another family.
And it was one stove.
It wasn't electric.
It was just awful.
And water was one.
It was in sink that time in ghetto.
And when it was-- it was hot, because we were crowded, and the people were fighting.
Maybe 10, or 12, or 14.
It was crowded.
And-- we were to washing-- or we'd-- it was difficult.
It's very, very difficult-- ghetto.
It wasn't a normal life.
No.
I didn't go to school.
I didn't do anything.
Well, but I didn't do anything.
Only the people who were working and they go.
German asked them to work, and they go out of the ghetto on that day.
Supervised by German.
And you can get some pass to go out of ghetto, but you have to get a pass.
I never go out of the ghetto myself.
I was in ghetto unless they told me to.
With this-- not by myself, but in the group to go somewhere.
In a group of people.
When did I live?
[SIGHS] Well, we lived-- we didn't live much after married, because he went to camp.
I was with my sister, and my sister-in-law, and the other sister-in-law.
That was all-- as they've been in other-- they lived in the other apartment in the same building, another apartment.
That I went upstairs to him.
It was up stairs.
Remember was one man.
And there was always parties up there.
They were drinking a lot.
Young Jewish people that were there.
And, well, I didn't-- I haven't been with him a lot.
But-- he wants to-- but I find out that I was pregnant in the ghetto, and I have abortion.
And it was very, very dangerous because I shouldn't.
I know that my sister gets some.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think every [INAUDIBLE] they cause some injection that I am-- the one Jewish woman doctor she did it for me.
And I was in the-- like a hospital.
They said that I got appendix or whatever.
Because if the German would find out, then-- There were not German all the time with us, you see.
They give this to Jewish people to look after us.
They give orders to the other one, to the other.
Higher, and higher, and higher.
It was bad.
It was very bad in the ghetto.
It was one day only.
The next day, they took him to Plaszow.
And I stay alone again, I mean, with my family.
But my father wasn't there already.
My sister.
I think only a few weeks.
Six or seven weeks, or maybe two months.
I think six weeks.
And then we have to go to Plaszow, to the camp.
They liquidated ghetto in part-- partly.
They start to [INAUDIBLE] with some group of people, then again, then again shifted to the camp.
Camp was in Plaszow, where they cross to Cracow.
And I wasn't to the very last woman in ghetto.
I was chosen in the middle to go out.
When did I leave?
Where?
You mean the date when I go?
Oh, well it was '43.
In February, or March, or something like this.
I don't know.
We married in January.
Or maybe on January, beginning of February-- I think in '43 I went to Plaszow.
I think that we have to walk.
I don't remember.
I think that we have to walk, and walk, and walk.
It was--
That wasn't very long.
The journey, you mean, to Plaszow?
Maybe one day.
One day only.
There was not much.
Yes, we did something, but we couldn't carry many things with us.
No.
When how we were ordered to leave everything in ghetto, we were ordered to give our jewelry, whoever have my-- it was a case.
And my sister had jewelry from my mother.
Diamond rings, and all this things, a whole bunch.
And she went-- to come the order to give the jewelry, she put in a jar with jam.
And-- but I was also frightened.
And when have the other order the death penalty-- that I took out, and I washed everything, and I gave it away.
I have the little earrings from my childhood that like-- forget-me-nots.
Blue and [INAUDIBLE].
And I took it, and whoever wanted that-- I don't know who was that situation.
And they pull it from my ear, the other one, because I couldn't take it out.
I have to give everything away.
Clothing, and fur coat, and everything.
We didn't have much.
No.
When we went to Plaszow, we really didn't have anything.
With whom, what-- I went with my sister, but in Plaszow they separate us.
In the beginning, I joined my husband.
And first few weeks, were women and men together, because Plaszow wasn't finished.
That was on so many thousand-- thousand of people.
I don't know how many people were over there.
Then the women went separate.
There was a banks [INAUDIBLE].
They built a banks from wood.
And we got a straw-- straw mattress from straw and some blankets.
And worst thing-- what the worst thing what I remember was the bugs, which would be beaten up bugs You know, little-- we call it [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH].
That doesn't exist in Australia.
That black-- black things that really stings so much that we have to scratch and scratch at night.
And we didn't have much sleep because of that bugs.
And that bugs was really tormenting me.
I remember this in beginning in ghetto.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And of course we have not enough food.
In Plaszow, I mean.
Not in ghetto.
In Plaszow start with that, because the ghetto was ghetto.
But Plaszow was much, much worse.
And start that-- then I was witnessing the execution part.
Yeah.
In Plaszow?
In the barracks.
You know what barracks is, yeah?
That was in the beginning, first few days, maybe I was with my husband.
Men and women were together when I arrived.
Then they separate.
Separate woman, separate for woman.
It was banks.
And I was sleeping in one bank with-- I don't know.
I don't remember even who it was.
A girl and-- And not with my sister.
My sister was somewhere else again in diffident barracks.
A plan?
I didn't think about plan.
What can we plan at that time?
We only think to be-- to be alive, not to be shot.
Because we've seen so many people perish.
So many people were shot, and tormented, and beaten.
In Plaszow, especially, there were the German [GERMAN], that were woman policeman, German.
And that was frightening.
There were-- one was named was Orlowska.
I would never forget her.
She was tall and blonde.
And she got a whip.
And once, I wanted to smuggle myself to my husband to men's quarter, and she caught me, and she beat me.
And I was bleeding, and I was blue.
[CRYING] Sorry.
[CRYING] OK.
[CRYING] OK.
I won't.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Don't ask me anymore.
OK.
That was the other one, small one.
We call her, [NON-ENGLISH], she had a crooked mouth, a crooked mouth.
And she likes girls.
She was sitting always with girls and petting-- petting the Jewish girls.
But that-- they was-- they was horrible that-- officer was awful.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SOBBING] Sorry.
I can't talk about.
I won't be long.
OK.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And Plaszow was awful.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because we seen it-- segregation.
They segregate people who are not looking well.
And you know they were-- they go to somewhere.
We didn't know they go to dead, but they were shooting people there.
And were police-- police-- Jewish policemen.
And where the high-class commander was [GERMAN] and his wife.
And they have special uniform.
They were beautiful uniform with high boots, and they have that strap [INAUDIBLE] or whatever you call it.
And they were going, and shouting, and swearing at us, and you have to listen.
And they were only-- only this sometimes.
And it the commander, Goeth.
Goeth was a-- it was really frightening because he got his gun out and he was shooting people.
Yes.
And one-- what I remember, when they hanged my cousin.
I have a cousin, my father's brother was in Plaszow.
Two brothers.
And one of the brother had a son, the only son.
And that boy was maybe 13 or 14.
And what I learned later that what he was whistling some song or whatever.
And how they call everybody on appellplatz and they will hang him.
It was a-- how you call it?
The [NON-ENGLISH], how you call it in English?
A gallows, yes?
And we have to witness this.
And-- it didn't hold it, and he fell down.
And he start to cry, and Goeth shoot him.
And they hang other people as well.
And-- oh, well.
That was-- it was frightening.
I couldn't think about anything.
It was frightening.
And we didn't have enough to eat.
We have in the morning, coffee.
With the tin cup.
And another couple [INAUDIBLE].
That was water and that was lunch time soup.
With the same dish we went, give me a quart of soup.
And we would really fight whoever would be last, because the soup sometimes have something thicker on the bottom.
That you want to be the last to get something more to eat.
And it's-- I want to-- I didn't want to tell you all of this.
That it-- sometime, I remember, we want to go to latrine.
Latrine was the worst thing.
The toilet-- it wasn't a toilet.
It was a big, big long wooden.
And one was sitting next to the other.
And I was-- it was so humiliating.
It was so awful, especially when I had period still at that time.
And we didn't have anything for this.
And it was really, really terrible.
I remember once I have-- I need to go pee.
And I peed to that dish.
And after, I have coffee with this-- [CRYING] I didn't told this anybody.
I don't know why I'm telling you.
I shouldn't have.
Anyhow, that was Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And anyhow, my husband and Henry were playing there for Goeth.
Because you know, they are musician.
And my sister-in-law, Henry's wife, was in different barrack.
She was leading the barrack like a-- all the-- you know someone who are leading.
I don't know how you call it.
My English now is very bad because I'm upset.
And we never knew.
And we heard that brother-in-law is to [INAUDIBLE] commandant.
And usually after it, the execution, they were called to play.
And we never knew if they were coming back or not.
They never knew this.
And then my husband met Schindler, I believe.
I never seen Schindler before Brunnlitz.
But my husband told me this, that-- that there was one German who was good.
And Schindler, as I believe, had a group of Jewish people in his factory outside the ghetto.
That I didn't know at that time about this.
And they start-- we heard something about list, lists, starting to make list.
And I'm tell you frank, I didn't know much about it, about that list, or whatever.
I didn't know much.
I knew that later I find out that somehow my sister was on the list as well.
But it was a German policeman one-- Goldberg, I think.
Marcel Goldberg who was the higher position.
Because they make it officer, and the Polish-- Polish policeman they make it normally are the higher.
The [GERMAN] was the commander.
And-- oh, yes.
Before this list was drawn, before this one they-- they shoot that [GERMAN] and his wife.
And [?
Monek ?] [?
Ferber, ?] I remember.
And-- and a few others who were the higher policemen.
They shoot them, and they put them down lying.
And we all have to march and looked at them.
But if somebody turned the head, they will shoot this person.
We don't-- we were not allowed not to look.
We have to look.
We just have to look at that dead body lying.
And was-- it was horrible.
And that was all Plaszow.
And-- and then, I remember how they take children.
They take children from their mother's arms.
And from that there was like a kindergarten or something.
God knows how.
I don't know what-- the children was separately.
And on-- on the big buggy or was it by horse?
I don't know.
I remember the children were screaming.
The mother was holding and screaming.
And music was playing.
And I don't know if my husband was playing or some record.
I don't know.
It was music playing.
And the scream of this children and the mothers.
And-- oh, that was-- that was awful.
I'm maybe mixing up things, but that was-- that was Plaszow.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then I find out that I have to go somewhere when my husband was going to Schindler.
I heard about Schindler.
Very little.
Very little.
I saw him in a beginning of the Plaszow.
He used to march to work somewhere outside.
Outside.
And it was wire.
And it wasn't an electric wire in the ghetto-- in the Plaszow.
It was just-- wire-- how you call it with this?
And I can go and see him through the wire.
And once, I even go close and we can kiss through the-- when he goes to work and back.
That's all what I-- I tired to see him, but I never could because that was not allowed to go to men's barrack.
Yes.
Yes.
I was working in garment shop.
That was a factory which repaired this uniform.
Germans' uniform.
And it was very-- first on the machine, it wasn't electric machine, that foot machine.
All [INAUDIBLE] garment shop that you were repairing.
There come a lot of-- a lot of uniform dirty, and with blood, and with lice that was.
And we have to work.
But and the other worse was the calling us to appellplatz to count us.
Comes very early in the morning.
Sometimes middle of the night we have to go.
Sit in-- stand in form for hours sometimes.
And-- and then we went back to barrack.
And we want to sleep, but the bugs they were eating us.
And it not enough sleep.
And I was working in the machine.
We made between us girls that someone can lie down in the corner and sleep a little bit.
But we have to say, [NON-ENGLISH], that we call it.
That someone is coming-- that quick, you have to get up.
And my head goes on the machine because I was so sleepy and so tired.
It loss of sleep and lack of food.
And that latrine, I remember that was hell.
That-- I never-- I never was alone for a moment.
Always with people around me.
We didn't-- we didn't have conversation.
We talked about not to be hungry, and to eat something more.
And to have something more, we got a little bit of bread.
We didn't know if keep it for later or eat it right now.
And what is-- and some were frightened that we are going to die anyhow.
We knew that we are going to be dead.
And some said, no, we are not.
And we didn't-- what would you talk about?
You couldn't talk about anything.
And I have a dream sometimes.
Maybe half the time when I was hungry, I had a dream that I would have something to eat.
Maybe soup or something.
Something from I remember from before the war.
But planning?
No, I couldn't plan anything.
Just to survive from day to day.
And where was your brother and sister?
My brother was in the Plaszow as well.
And he was one of the policeman.
He was a Jewish policeman.
Yes.
Because he'd come to the army.
Well, there have to be Jewish policemen because the German demand this.
And they choose the people who were just fit for this.
And he came from the army, and he was Jewish, but-- But I know that he helps many people.
He really helps many people.
And people didn't work, and he smacks them sometimes.
He saved their lives.
Because if German come, they would shoot him.
But people didn't understand this.
Some of them had-- [INAUDIBLE] all their life about that Jewish policemen.
But some of them were just doing that-- they have to do it.
Of course, all people are different.
Some are better, some were good.
That's it.
But I know when my husband hadn't done anything really wrong.
He was--
OK.
